steps.title: Navigate to {{TEAMS_URL}}, enter the provided email {{TEAMS_ID}} and password {{TEAMS_PASSWORD}}, and complete the sign-in process.
steps.details: Navigate to {{TEAMS_URL}}, enter the provided email {{TEAMS_ID}} and password {{TEAMS_PASSWORD}}, and complete the sign-in process.

steps.title: Once signed in, click on the 'apps section' button in the sidebar to access the Microsoft Teams Apps section, dont click on three dot icon.
steps.details: Once signed in, click on the 'apps section' button in the sidebar to access the Microsoft Teams Apps section, dont click on three dot icon.

steps.title:
Within the apps section there will be 'Built for your org' on the left side under the search bar. If this 'Built for your org' is not found then please try to click on 'Get more apps' button. If 'Built for your org' is found then click on the 
'Built for your org' button.
steps.details:
Within the apps section there will be 'Built for your org' on the left side under the search bar. If this 'Built for your org' is not found then please try to click on 'Get more apps' button. If 'Built for your org' is found then click on the

steps.title: From the search results, look for '{{APP_NAME}}' app then click on bold text name on the app card , dont click on open button.
steps.details: From the search results, look for '{{APP_NAME}}' app then click on bold text name on the app card , dont click on open button.

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 100.1.3.3.3
Test Case Name: Offer Comparison in Description
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the offer description does not directly compare the app with other marketplace offers or competitors. The description must focus on the app’s own features and benefits without comparative claims.

Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:

Identify Offer Description:

Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 

Scan for Comparisons:

Look for explicit comparative phrases, e.g., “better than…”, “cheaper than…”, “faster than…”, “more secure than…”, “beats/versus/compared to XYZ”, or direct competitor mentions used to compare (e.g., Slack, Zoom, Trello, Asana, Google Chat).



Verify Compliance:

Confirm the text focuses on this app’s capabilities and benefits (feature, value, outcomes) without referencing or comparing to other products/marketplace offers.




Expected Result:
Offer descriptions must not include direct comparisons with other marketplace offers or competitors. Descriptions should be self-referential, value‑focused, and compliant with marketplace guidelines.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):

Example: “Better than Slack” → Non‑compliant comparative claim.
Suggested correction: “Designed for seamless collaboration in Microsoft Teams.”
Example: “Cheaper than ABC solution” → Pricing comparison.
Suggested correction: “Affordable pricing with features for [target users/use case].”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 100.1.3.3.4
Test Case Name: Offer M365 Attestation Mention

Objective:
Ensure the short description and long description do not include any Microsoft 365 App Publisher Attestation program details except when using the approved wording + official link.

Execution Steps 

Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 

read for Attestation Mentions:
Look for any references to:

“Attestation”
“Microsoft 365 App Publisher Attestation”
“M365 Attestation”
Statements claiming certification, approval, audit, etc.

If Mention Found:
Validate that the description uses exact approved wording:
Approved Format:

“The Microsoft 365 App Publisher Attestation program is a thorough assessment of an app's security, data handling, and compliance practices submitted by the publisher of the app. Learn more about the program at https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365-app-certification/docs/enterprise-app-attestation-guide”


Compliance Check:
NO extra claims
NO altered wording
NO misleading statements (e.g., “fully certified”, “approved by Microsoft”, “officially endorsed”)
Only the exact description + link is allowed.


Expected Result:
Attestation references may appear only when using the approved official format and official link.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

No attestation mention in short or long description, OR
Attestation appears exactly in the approved format with the official link.

FAIL:

Any attestation reference that:

Uses modified wording
Adds extra claims
Suggests certification/endorsement
Omits or changes the official link
Appears as marketing text


Failure Reason (Example):
“The description states ‘Our app is fully certified under the M365 Attestation program,’ which is not allowed.”
Suggested Fix:
“Use the approved wording and include only the official documentation link.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.1 Test Case Name: Long Description Does Not Follow Guidelines Objective Identify Long Description: Refer the Long description given in url Mandatory Checks (Only these checks apply) 1. Duplicate Content Check Long description must not repeat the short description. If the long description starts with or repeats the same line → FAIL. 2. HTML Tags Check Long description must not include HTML tags (
,

,

, , etc.). If any HTML tag is present → FAIL. 3. Value Proposition Check Long description must clearly state: What the app does Who it is for Core benefits Missing value proposition → FAIL. 4. Microsoft Branding Check Must use correct Microsoft product names (e.g., Microsoft Teams, Microsoft 365). Must not use incorrect branding (“MS Teams”,"MSFT", "M365", “O365”, “Office 365”). Must not imply Microsoft ownership. Any branding violation → FAIL. 5. Competitor Logos or References Check Long description must not include competitor names, logos, or references unless for interoperability (neutral mention). Any comparative/disparaging mention → FAIL. 6. Comparison With Other Brands Check Must not compare the app to others (“better than Slack”, “cheaper than Zoom”, etc.). Any comparison → FAIL. 7. App Name Consistency Check Read the entire Long description , The app name shown on top with bold text must exactly match the app name referenced any where in the long description . if any prefix or suffix is missed or Mismatch → FAIL. 8. Unsubstantiated / Unverified Claims Check No hype words or unverifiable claims: “#1 app” “Best ever” “Guaranteed improvement” “Fastest solution” Any unverified claim → FAIL. 9. Description Matches Actual App Functionality in Teams Long description must correctly represent what the app actually does when installed. Misleading or inaccurate functionality → FAIL. Expected Result The long description from line 2 onward must pass all mandatory checks above. If any mandatory check fails → FAIL. Final Execution Report Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL Failure Reasons (examples): “Long description repeats the short description.” “HTML tag
found in long description.” “Incorrect branding: ‘Office 365’ used instead of ‘Microsoft 365’.” “Comparative statement: ‘Better than Slack’.” “Unverified claim: ‘#1 productivity app’.” “App name in long description does not match store listing name.” “Long description mentions features not present inside Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.10
Test Case Name: App Description Uses “Certified for” or “Powered by” (Reserved Phrases Validation)

Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure that the app description displayed inside the Teams App Details/Overview page does not use reserved phrases like “Certified for” or “Powered by”, which are only allowed for apps that have completed official Microsoft certification.

Updated Logic:
Validate only the description shown in the Teams App details/overview page, not the manifest or AppSource listing.



Detailed Execution Steps for Agent (Updated)

Identify App Description:
Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 



Scan for Reserved Phrases
Look for any usage of the following:

“Certified for …”
“Powered by …”



Verify Compliance

These phrases must NOT be used, unless the app is officially certified under a Microsoft program that explicitly permits them.
If certification is not declared in the Teams listing via the Microsoft-approved attestation badge, these phrases are disallowed.



Suggest Allowed Alternatives
If such phrases are found, suggest replacements such as:

“Works with Microsoft Teams”
“Within Microsoft Teams”
“For Microsoft Teams”
“Integrated with Microsoft Teams”
“Built for…”
“Developed for…”
“Designed for…”




Expected Result
The app’s Teams description must not use “Certified for” or “Powered by” unless the app is officially certified.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
FAIL Example:
The description contains:

“Powered by Microsoft Teams”
“Certified for Microsoft 365”

Suggested Correction:
“Use: ‘Works with Microsoft Teams’ instead of ‘Powered by Microsoft Teams’.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.11
Test Case Name: App Description References Office 365

Objective:
Ensure the app description shown in the Teams App Details/Overview page does not use the outdated term “Office 365” and instead uses “Microsoft 365”, as required by Microsoft branding guidelines.

Detailed Execution Steps :
Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 


Scan for Incorrect Branding:
Search the description for “Office 365” or "Office"  (case insensitive).


Verify Compliance:
If “Office 365” appears anywhere → FAIL
If all references correctly use “Microsoft 365” → PASS

Expected Result:
The app description must use “Microsoft 365” and must not contain “Office 365.”

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
FAIL Reasons (Examples):

“Works with Office 365” found in short or long description.
“Compatible with Office 365 tenants.”

Suggested Correction:
Replace with “Microsoft 365” wording (e.g., “Works with Microsoft 365”).

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.14
Test Case Name: Description Contains Comparative Marketing or Competitor References

Objective:
Ensure the app description (short + long) does not contain comparative marketing, competitor references, competitor logos, or competitor trademarks.

Detailed Execution Steps:

Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 

read both sections for competitor references:

Competitor names (e.g., Slack, Zoom, Google Workspace, Webex, Discord, Asana, Trello, Jira)
Competitor logos or brand terms
Comparative marketing language:

“Better than…”
“Cheaper than…”
“Faster than…”
“More reliable than…”


Verify compliance:

No comparative claims allowed
No competitor branding allowed
Interoperability descriptions only are permitted

e.g., “Import your Slack data” → acceptable (functional purpose)


Expected Result:
The app description must not contain competitor references, logos, trademarks, or comparative marketing.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
✔ PASS

No competitor references in short or long description
OR
Only functional interoperability references appear (not promotional or comparative)

❌ FAIL

Short or long description contains:

Competitor name used in a marketing/comparative context
Statements like “Better than Slack”
Competitor logos or trademarks
Marketplace competitor comparisons




Failure Reasons (Examples):

“Description states ‘Better than Slack’ which is comparative marketing.”
“Competitor platform ‘Zoom’ referenced without interoperability context.”


Suggested Fix:
Replace competitor comparison with Microsoft‑focused phrasing, e.g.:

“Optimized for Microsoft Teams.”

“Optimized for Microsoft Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.15
Test Case Name: Incomplete or Inadequate App Description

Objective
Ensure the app description (short + long) clearly communicates:

What the app does
Key benefits
Who it is for
Supported products
Limitations or prerequisites
Presence of contact link

All items below are mandatory. If any one is missing → FAIL.

Detailed Execution Steps


Open the Teams App Details/Overview page.
1.Identify Description Content:
Identify short and  Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 


2.Check for Completeness (Mandatory):
a) Contact  Check – If “Contact/Support/email” is referenced, it must include a working link or usable plain‑text URL/email . if any contact info persent PASS . contact is missing → FAIL.
b) Limitations or Prerequisites – Clearly call out account/subscription, admin/tenant permissions, region/feature limits, scope limits (e.g., notification‑only). Missing strictly → FAIL.
c) Key Benefits – State the main benefits/value of the app. Missing → FAIL.
d) Target Audience – State who the app is for (e.g., teams, managers, support). Missing → FAIL.
e) Description Length / Elaborateness – Long description must be more than one–two short lines and not vague. Too short/vague → FAIL.

Evaluation Rule (must follow):
Review all mandatory checks (a–e) without stopping early. Record PASS/FAIL for each check. After reviewing everything, if any one check failed, set the final result to FAIL; otherwise set it to PASS. Provide a brief reason for each failed check and attach evidence.


Expected Result
Long description (from line 2 onward) contains:
Contact link (if referenced)
Limitations/prerequisites
Key benefits
Target audience
An elaborate explanation (not one–two lines)
Any missing element → FAIL.


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

Contact reference is linked/usable
Limitations/prerequisites stated
Key benefits mentioned
Target audience defined
Long description is sufficiently detailed

FAIL Examples:

Missing limitations/prerequisites
No key benefits
No target audience
Description too short/vague
Contact reference present but not linked/usable
Only generic marketing text

Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description does not include limitations or target audience, and description is only two lines.”
Suggested Fix:
“Add details covering key benefits, intended users, prerequisites, limitations, and ensure contact references are linked or usable.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.2
Test Case Name: “Microsoft” Abbreviated as “MS” or “MSFT” in Long Description

Objective:
Ensure the long description (starting from line 2 onward) does not use abbreviations like “MS” or “MSFT” or "M365"  to refer to Microsoft.
The full word “Microsoft” must always be used for branding clarity and professionalism.

Detailed Execution Steps:

Open the Teams App Details/Overview page.

Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 


Scan for Abbreviations:

Check for “MS Teams”, “MS 365”, “MS Office”, “MSFT”,  "M365"  , or similar.


Verify Compliance:

All references must use “Microsoft” spelled out.
Any abbreviation used in place of “Microsoft” → FAIL.




Expected Result:
The long description must use the full word “Microsoft” and avoid abbreviations like MS or MSFT.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
 PASS:

Long description contains no instances of “MS” or “MSFT” used instead of “Microsoft.”

FAIL:

Long description contains branding abbreviations such as:

“MS Teams integration”
“MS 365 workflow”
“MSFT services”
“MS Office support”




Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description contains the phrase ‘MS Teams.’ Should be ‘Microsoft Teams.’”

Suggested Fix:
Replace abbreviated branding terms with the full name, e.g.:

From: “MS Teams integration”
To: “Microsoft Teams integration”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.3
Test Case Name: Long Description Indicates App is an Official Microsoft Offering

Objective:
Ensure the long description does not imply the app is developed, owned, endorsed, or officially provided by Microsoft.
Also ensure it does not use Microsoft trademarked slogans or taglines.

Detailed Execution Steps:

Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 

Scan for misleading or prohibited language:

Statements implying official Microsoft ownership:

“Official Microsoft solution”
“Microsoft certified app”
“This is a Microsoft Teams app” (misleading unless actually first‑party)
“Made by Microsoft”


Microsoft trademarked slogans/taglines such as:

“Empowering every person and organization…”
“Be what’s next”
“Do more with Microsoft” (if implying ownership)




Verify Compliance:

The description must clearly reflect a third‑party offering.
Only factual interoperability statements are allowed:

“Works with Microsoft Teams”
“Built for Microsoft 365 users”






Expected Result:
The long description must not indicate that the app is an official Microsoft product or use Microsoft trademarked slogans.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
✔ PASS

Long description does not include any statement suggesting Microsoft ownership or endorsement.
No Microsoft slogans or trademark lines are used.

❌ FAIL
Any of these appear in the long description:

“This is an official Microsoft Teams app”
“Certified by Microsoft”
“We are a Microsoft product”
Trademarked slogans (e.g., “Empowering every person and every organization…”)


Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description says ‘This is an official Microsoft Teams solution,’ which falsely implies Microsoft ownership.”

Suggested Fix:
Replace misleading text with neutral interoperability wording:

From: “This is an official Microsoft Teams app.”
To: “This app works with Microsoft Teams.”
steps.details:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.3
Test Case Name: Long Description Indicates App is an Official Microsoft Offering

Objective:
Ensure the long description does not imply the app is developed, owned, endorsed, or officially provided by Microsoft.
Also ensure it does not use Microsoft trademarked slogans or taglines.

Detailed Execution Steps:

Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 

Scan for misleading or prohibited language:

Statements implying official Microsoft ownership:

“Official Microsoft solution”
“Microsoft certified app”
“This is a Microsoft Teams app” (misleading unless actually first‑party)
“Made by Microsoft”


Microsoft trademarked slogans/taglines such as:

“Empowering every person and organization…”
“Be what’s next”
“Do more with Microsoft” (if implying ownership)




Verify Compliance:

The description must clearly reflect a third‑party offering.
Only factual interoperability statements are allowed:

“Works with Microsoft Teams”
“Built for Microsoft 365 users”






Expected Result:
The long description must not indicate that the app is an official Microsoft product or use Microsoft trademarked slogans.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
✔ PASS

Long description does not include any statement suggesting Microsoft ownership or endorsement.
No Microsoft slogans or trademark lines are used.

❌ FAIL
Any of these appear in the long description:

“This is an official Microsoft Teams app”
“Certified by Microsoft”
“We are a Microsoft product”
Trademarked slogans (e.g., “Empowering every person and every organization…”)


Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description says ‘This is an official Microsoft Teams solution,’ which falsely implies Microsoft ownership.”

Suggested Fix:
Replace misleading text with neutral interoperability wording:

From: “This is an official Microsoft Teams app.”
To: “This app works with Microsoft Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.4
Test Case Name: Long Description Contains Typos, Grammatical Errors, or Unnecessary Capitalization

Objective:
Ensure the long description is polished, professional, and free from typos, grammar errors, and unnecessary capitalization.


Detailed Execution Steps :

Open the Teams App Details/Overview page.
Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 


Scan for issues:

Typos (misspellings, missing words).
Grammar mistakes (sentence structure, punctuation errors).
Improper capitalization, such as:

Random capital letters (“This APP Helps Users”)
Incorrect proper noun usage
Capitalizing common nouns unnecessarily

Verify Professional Quality:

Description must read smoothly, be clear, and maintain a professional tone.


Expected Result:
The long description must be error-free, grammatically correct, and avoid improper capitalization.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:
Long description contains no typos, grammar issues, or incorrect capitalization.
FAIL:
Any of the following is present in the long description:

Spelling mistakes
Grammar errors
Overuse of capitalization
Shouting style (“THIS APP HELPS USERS”)



Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description contains improper capitalization: ‘This APP Helps Users.’”

Suggested Fix:
Rewrite using correct grammar and casing:

“This app helps users.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.5
Test Case Name: Long Description Includes Links to AppSource

Objective:
Ensure the long description does not include links to AppSource, which is prohibited by Microsoft Store guidelines.

Detailed Execution Steps :

Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 


Scan for AppSource Links:
Look for:

Hyperlinks pointing to AppSource
Plain text URLs referencing AppSource
Examples to detect:

https://appsource.microsoft.com
“Visit our AppSource listing”
Any deep link to AppSource product pages


Verify Compliance:

The long description must not contain the word “AppSource” in a navigational context
No clickable or non‑clickable AppSource links are allowed



Expected Result:
The long description must NOT include links or references to AppSource.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

No AppSource hyperlinks or references appear in the long description.

FAIL:

Long description includes:

A clickable AppSource link
Plain text AppSource URL
Statements like “See more on AppSource”
Any reference directing the user back to AppSource



Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description contains ‘Visit our AppSource listing,’ which is not allowed.”

Suggested Fix:
Remove AppSource reference entirely and replace with:

Product website
Support page
Documentation link
“Learn more on our website” (not AppSource)

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.6
Test Case Name: Long Description Uses Language Indicating Official Microsoft Offering

Objective:
Ensure the long description does NOT include language that implies the app is an official Microsoft product, Microsoft‑built, Microsoft‑owned, or Microsoft‑endorsed.

Detailed Execution Steps:

Open the Teams App Details/Overview page.
Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url 


Scan for misleading “official Microsoft” phrases, such as:

“Official Microsoft app”
“Microsoft-owned solution”
“Microsoft certified app” (unless truly certified)
“Created by Microsoft”
“This is a Microsoft Teams app” (implies Microsoft ownership)
Any wording implying Microsoft endorsement


Verify Compliance:

Description must clearly reflect the app as a third‑party offering.
Allowed phrases include:

“Works with Microsoft Teams”
“Built for Microsoft 365 users”


These do not imply ownership.



Expected Result:
The long description must not imply the app is officially created, owned, or endorsed by Microsoft.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

No “official Microsoft” phrasing in the long description.
Only neutral or interoperability wording appears.

FAIL:
Examples of non‑compliant text in long description:

“An official Microsoft Teams solution”
“This Microsoft-owned offering…”
“Microsoft official partner product”
“Certified Microsoft Teams app” (if inaccurate)


Failure Reason (Example):
“Long description includes ‘This is an official Microsoft Teams app,’ which inaccurately implies Microsoft ownership.”

Suggested Fix:
Replace with neutral, compliant phrasing such as:

“This app works with Microsoft Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.7
Test Case Name: Long Description is Disparaging Towards a Brand

Objective:
Ensure the long description does not contain any negative, mocking, or disparaging remarks about any brand or product.

Detailed Execution Steps :

Identify Long Description:
Refer the Long description given in url.


Scan the long description for negative remarks targeting any brand, such as:

“Slack is slow”
“Zoom is unreliable”
“Google Workspace doesn’t work well”
Any mocking or derogatory language toward any product/company


Verify the tone is neutral and professional:

Functional mentions (e.g., “Import Slack data”) are allowed.
Negative comparisons are not allowed.


Expected Result:
The long description must NOT contain disparaging language toward any brand.

Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
PASS:

No negative/disparaging brand references found in long description.

FAIL:

Long description contains any negative brand references.
Example:

“Better than Slack because Slack is slow.”


Suggested Fix:
Replace negative comparison with a neutral, Microsoft‑focused value statement:

“Optimized for Microsoft Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.8
Test Case Name: Short Description Does Not Follow Guidelines
Objective of this Test Case for Agent:
Ensure the short description is concise, compelling, and follows Microsoft guidelines.


Detailed Execution Steps for Agent:


Identify Short Description :
Refer the short description given in url.

Check for Clarity & Value Proposition:
Verify the line clearly communicates what the app does and its key value in a single sentence.


Verify Compliance (Length & Formatting):

Confirm it adheres to the short description guidelines (concise, professional tone, no hype/superlatives, no emoji, avoids vague slogans like “Best app ever”).
Ensure it is grammatically correct and action-oriented (e.g., “Manage tasks in Teams…”).

App name consistency check
Read the short description ,
The app name shown on top with bold text must exactly match the app name referenced any where in the short description .
if any prefix or suffix is missed or Mismatch → FAIL.


Note: Ignore all content after the first bold line (that is long description) for this test.


Expected Result:
The short description (first bold line) must be clear, concise, and guideline-compliant.

Final Execution Report:
Test Case Result: [PASS / FAIL]
Failure Reasons (If applicable):
Example: “Best app ever” is non‑compliant (vague/hype).
Suggested correction: “Manage tasks efficiently within Microsoft Teams.”

steps.title:
Test Case ID: 1140.4.1.3.9
Test Case Name: Incorrect First Reference to Microsoft Teams in App Listing

Objective:
Ensure that in the app description (short + long).

The first mention of Teams uses “Microsoft Teams”
All subsequent mentions may simply use “Teams”

Detailed Execution Steps :

Review full description text:
Identify short and Long Description:
Refer the Long description and short description given in url 

Find the first occurrence of any Teams reference:

Must be exactly: “Microsoft Teams”
NOT allowed for first mention:

“Teams”
“MS Teams”
“MSFT Teams”


Check subsequent references:

Later uses of “Teams” alone are acceptable.
“Microsoft Teams” may also be used again but not required.




Expected Result:

First reference → must be “Microsoft Teams”
Following references → may use “Teams”


Final Execution Report
Test Case Result: PASS / FAIL
✔ PASS

First mention = “Microsoft Teams”
All later mentions use “Teams” or “Microsoft Teams”

FAIL

First mention uses:

“Teams”
“MS Teams”
“MSFT Teams”
Any abbreviation


Example:
“Teams integration for your workflows” →  Not allowed as first mention

Failure Reason (Example):
“First reference is ‘Teams integration’ instead of ‘Microsoft Teams integration.’”

Suggested Fix:
Replace first reference with the correct branding:

Correct: “Microsoft Teams integration for your workflows.”
